channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: Far Crimea: war comes to Russiaâs door
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: SpaceXâs volatile valuation and remembering Alan Greenspan

item.title: The US in Brief: The nine and the one
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: Venezuela suffers its worst earthquake in a century
item.description: The impoverished country is struggling to cope with the damage from two nearly simultaneous shocks

item.title: Where will Europeâs heatwave be most deadly?
item.description: We analyse 854 cities to see which will be worst affected

item.title: Why the global elite are collecting citizenships
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, how to buy a passport

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
Eight of the best new novels to read this summer
item.description: Many of our selections explore family secrets

item.title: Why the market for histories of depressions is red hot
item.description: â1873â, a new book, offers some lessons for the AI craze

item.title: Is America becoming a gerontocracy?
item.description: A new book spots a real problem and offers terrible solutions

item.title: Teaching AI how people work is fraught with problems
item.description: Tacit knowledge is vital to many jobs

item.title: Why easyJet may be heading for a break-up
item.description: Its private-equity suitor may be looking to sell it for parts

item.title: UPS is losing ground to FedEx
item.description: The two logistics giants have similar strategiesâand face a common threat

item.title: Students are doing worse than you think
item.description: Some at college or university are testing no better than ten-year-olds

item.title: Two state elections may break Malaysiaâs ruling coalition
item.description: Anwar Ibrahimâs long-sought tenure as prime minister may be cut short

item.title: Asian governments are making children care for their parents
item.description: As populations age, the elderly are being neglected

item.title: America is using force to drive gangsters from Venezuelaâs gold
item.description: It wonât be a pushover

item.title: Bangladeshâs main industry is battered by blackouts and rising costs
item.description: A crisis may loom

item.title: Two powerful earthquakes hit Venezuela
item.description: Such tightly sequenced quakes are rare

item.title: Medieval-style fortifications are back in the Sahel
item.description: Newly walled towns are a sign of shrivelling state authority

item.title: The dramatic Trumpification of Latin America
item.description: Real problems, copycattery and a network of hacks have helped Donald Trumpâs pals win a string of elections

item.title: Does Gadi Eisenkot offer Israel a new chance?
item.description: Israelis think he may be a genuine alternative to Binyamin Netanyahu

item.title: Ebola has put Africaâs fragile health systems in the spotlight
item.description: Emergencies and aid cuts are accelerating a push for self-reliance

item.title: What a surge of anti-migrant protests says about South Africa
item.description: It is too easy for xenophobic vigilantes to take advantage of poverty and a weak state

item.title: J.D. Vance is heir to a more radical politics than Trumpism
item.description: â¦if, of course, he believes what heâs saying

item.title: Americaâs pro-Israel lobby considers next steps
item.description: Israelâs defenders are flailing at the White House and at the ballot box

item.title: Democrats and Republicans agree: AI is scary
item.description: Itâs about the only thing that unites them

item.title: The EU is just too damn slow
item.description: Why Brussels needs to pick up the pace

item.title: Franceâs south is testing out hard-right rule
item.description: The National Rally is mixing raw nativism with promises of fiscal liberalism

item.title: Why Germanyâs cities are going broke
item.description: Bad news for residents, good news for the far rightâs electoral chances

item.title: Peter Magyar is trying to fire up Hungarian democracy
item.description: A constitutional amendment would burn away the remnants of Viktor Orbanâs regime

item.title: Slipping up or down the class system is surprisingly bearable
item.description: Much better than being solidly working-class

item.title: Lessons from the childhood home of Britainâs probable next leader
item.description: Warrington is less glamorous than Manchester, but stranger

item.title: The BBC switches off its oldest service
item.description: Long-wave radioâs time is up. Terrestrial television will surely follow

item.title: AI modelsâ values are very different from most peopleâs
item.description: They are more secular and more liberalâunless theyâre made in China

item.title: What to do about space debris?
item.description: Also this week, streaming music, breastfeeding, Antoni GaudÃ­, taking advice from uncles, corporate reading lists

item.title: Latin America has turned Trumpy. That creates opportunities
item.description: Never before have the politics of the region shifted so quickly

item.title: University-for-all harms poor students the most
item.description: Time for sane standards in higher education

item.title: With Iran emboldened, its neighbours must put old divisions aside
item.description: With a common foe, they cannot afford petty feuds
